Neurological History.prompt: Provide a detailed neurological history for this individual, including onset, progression, and characteristics of symptoms, past medical and surgical history, family history of neurological conditions, medications, social history, and any other relevant factors that could inform a neurological assessment.

levels.2: Obtains a complete and relevant neurologic history
levels.3: Obtains an organized neurologic history, including collateral information as appropriate
levels.4: Efficiently obtains an organized, hypothesis-driven neurologic history
levels.5: Serves as a role model in obtaining a hypothesis-driven neurologic history

Imaging.prompt: Imaging Studies: Interpret any provided imaging studies (CT or MRI), including identification of any abnormalities, assessment of brain structures, signs of pathology, and potential etiologies.

levels.1: Identifies basic neuroanatomy on brain and vascular anatomy of the head and neck magnetic resonance (MR) and computed tomography (CT)
levels.2: Identifies major abnormalities of the brain and cerebrovascular system on MR and CT; Identifies basic anatomy of the spine and spinal cord on MR and CT
levels.3: Identifies typical abnormalities of the brain and cerbrovascular system on MR and CT; Identifies abnormalities of the spine and spinal cord on MR and CT
levels.4: Identifies subtle abnormalities of brain and cerbrovascular system on MR and CT; Interprets MR and CT of the spine

EEG.prompt: Interpret the provided EEG studies, noting any significant findings and their possible clinical implications.

levels.1: Identifies patients for whom EEG is appropriate
levels.2: Recognizes normal EEG features, including common artifacts, in children and adults
levels.3: Recognizes patterns of status epilepticus, normal EEG variants, and common abnormalities in children and adults
levels.4: Interprets common EEG abnormalities and patterns that could represent status epilepticus
levels.5: Interprets uncommon EEG abnormalities and creates a report

Differential Diagnosis.prompt: Provide an assessment of this individual's cognitive status, including the degree of impairment, potential contributing etiologies (if applicable), a complete differential diagnosis, and any relevant contributing factors.

levels.1: Identifies typical presentations of commonly encountered neurologic conditions
levels.2: Diagnoses commonly encountered neurologic conditions; Develops an initial treatment plan for commonly encountered neurologic disorders
levels.3: Identifies atypical presentations of commonly encountered neurologic conditions; Individualizes management and follow-up plan for commonly encountered neurologic disorders, considering risks, benefits, and non-pharmacologic strategies
levels.4: Diagnoses uncommon neurologic conditions; Adapts management plan based upon patient response and complications of therapy; Identifies when to change acuity of care
levels.5: Identifies atypical presentations of uncommon neurologic conditions; Longitudinally manages uncommon neurologic conditions

levels.1: Identifies typical presentations of commonly encountered neurologic conditions
levels.2: Diagnoses commonly encountered neurologic conditions; Develops an initial treatment plan for commonly encountered neurologic disorders
levels.3: Identifies atypical presentations of commonly encountered neurologic conditions; Individualizes management plan, ensuring the appropriate level of care throughout hospitalization and upon discharge
levels.4: Diagnoses uncommon neurologic conditions; Adapts management plan based upon treatment response, disease progression, and complications of therapy
levels.5: Identifies atypical presentations of uncommon neurologic conditions; Leads the management of patients with complex and uncommon neurologic conditions

levels.1: Discusses a general diagnostic approach appropriate to clinical presentation
levels.2: Lists indications, contraindications, risks, and benefits of diagnostic testing
levels.3: Prioritizes and interprets diagnostic tests appropriate to clinical urgency and complexity
levels.4: Uses complex diagnostic approaches in uncommon situations
levels.5: Demonstrates sophisticated knowledge of diagnostic testing and controversies

levels.1: Recognizes contributions of common psychiatric disorders and their treatment to neurologic diseases
levels.2: Develops a treatment plan that considers psychiatric comorbidities and side effects of psychiatric medications
levels.3: Accurately differentiates psychiatric or functional contributions to neurologic symptoms
levels.4: Leads a discussion with a patient and/or caregiver that explains the psychiatric or functional contribution to the patient’s neurologic symptoms
levels.5: Develops a shared management plan that addresses the psychiatric or functional contribution to neurologic symptoms
